name: The marital dispute and the verse of choice
epithet: One month apart, and a choice between two worlds
title: The marital dispute and the verse of choice: when the Prophet ﷺ withdrew for a month
meta_description: Day 98 of the seerah: the one month the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ withdrew from his wives, the bare attic room, and the verse of choice in Surah al-Ahzab, retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's series.
keywords: seerah, Prophet Muhammad, marital dispute, ila, verse of choice, Surah al-Ahzab, at-Tahrim, Aisha, Hafsa, Umar ibn al-Khattab, mothers of the believers, Yasir Qadhi seerah

video.title: Seerah of Prophet Muhammad 98 - Marital dispute with the Wives ~ Dr. Yasir Qadhi | 3rd December 2014

source_note: Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 98: the marital dispute and the verse of choice (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an: Sahih International, verified via quran.ai. The narration is the Sheikh's, the phrasing is Buruja's.
lede: There is one season in the whole of his ﷺ life when the dispute was serious enough that he withdrew from his own home. One. In fourteen centuries of being held up as the perfect husband, this is the single time it ever reached that point, and Allah sent down Qur'an because of it. So we approach it the way Dr. Yasir Qadhi approaches it: gently, and with our eyes open to the humanity of everyone in the story.

Because that is the first lesson of the day. The Prophet ﷺ is the best of all men, but he is a man. His wives are the best of all women, the mothers of the believers, but they are wives in a marriage. What they wanted was permitted. What he chose was higher. And out of that gap came a month of distance and a verse that still asks every reader to choose.

sections.h2: What the mothers of the believers asked for
paras: For most of his life there was almost nothing to ask for. He ﷺ was born an orphan, worked as a shepherd for pennies, lived crowded in the house of Abu Talib, and did not so much as own a riding animal until the Hijrah. Then, slowly, the wealth of the ummah began to rise: the ransoms after Badr, the lands of Khaybar, the gift of Fadak that fed his household, the vast spoils of Hunayn from which he kept not a single coin for himself.

And as the believers around them began to live a little more comfortably, his wives, may Allah be pleased with them, asked for the same. A better house, perhaps, better furnishings, a share of the standard rising all around them. The exact words were never recorded, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi says it is right that they were not: this was the private speech of a home, and no one should write it down.

Hear this clearly, because it is the heart of the episode. What they asked for was not a sin. It was not even disliked. It was halal, the most natural human wish in the world, and wanting it took nothing away from their noble rank. Two of the wives gathered the others into a shared request, the way any case is stronger when more than one voice carries it. They were asking their husband, who genuinely had access to wealth, for a fair portion of it. Nothing in the story diminishes them. The weight of it falls entirely on a man who had decided, for our sake, to live with less.

The story comes to us mostly in the first person, from Umar ibn al-Khattab, radiyallahu anhu, because two of the wives at the center of it were Hafsa, his daughter, and Aisha. Years earlier his son-in-law Ibn Abbas had been desperate to ask him about all of this, and waited over a year for the right moment before Umar finally told him the whole of it.

Umar set the scene with something that had unsettled him. In Makkah, he said, we were men who dominated our women. Then we came to Madinah and found a people whose women answered back, and our women learned it from theirs. One day he rebuked his own wife for replying to him, and she shot back: why are you surprised? The wives of the Prophet ﷺ answer him too, and sometimes one of them will keep her distance from him for a whole day.

It stopped him cold. He went straight to Hafsa: do you answer him back? Do you leave him for the day? Yes, she said. Both. And here Dr. Yasir Qadhi asks you not to flinch but to feel the humanity of it. This is the picture of a real marriage, with its back-and-forth, its quiet half-days, its ordinary friction, and a husband ﷺ who tolerated all of it without ever once imposing the harsh customs he had grown up with. Things are permitted to his wives that would be unthinkable from anyone else, precisely because they are his wives. He told Hafsa, frightened for her: do not let the standing of your companion fool you. Aisha is more beloved to him than you are; she can carry what you cannot.

Then one night, after the city had gone dark, Umar's neighbor came pounding on his door. Umar rushed out certain of the worst the times could hold: have the Ghassanids attacked? No, the man said. Something worse has happened. The Messenger of Allah ﷺ has divorced his wives.

Sit with that for a moment. For these Companions, an enemy army on the march was the lesser disaster. A crack in the household of the Prophet ﷺ was the greater one, because they loved him so completely that his private grief outweighed any public danger. By morning the rumor had swept the whole of Madinah. Umar came to the masjid after Fajr and found the people gathered, turning pebbles over in their hands, waiting, weeping, an entire community undone by the thought that his ﷺ home might be breaking.

He went to the homes first. He found Hafsa in tears and rebuked her hard: did I not warn you? He found that the Prophet ﷺ had not gone to any of his wives at all. He had withdrawn alone to a small chamber above the masjid, reached by a ladder, a bare little room he otherwise almost never used.

Umar asked permission to enter, three times, before it was granted, and he made sure his purpose was understood: he had not come as a father-in-law to plead for Hafsa. If the Prophet ﷺ commanded him to strike off her head, he said, he would obey. He came only as a friend, to learn what had happened.

Inside, this is the scene that has reached every Muslim child since. A handful of barley. A half-tanned skin of water. A vessel in the corner, and almost nothing else. The Prophet ﷺ was lying on a mat woven from palm fiber, and when he turned to face Umar, the rough weave had pressed its marks into his side. The richest man in standing on the face of the earth, and his bed had left lines in his skin. Umar wept.

His first question was the only one that mattered: have you divorced your wives? No, the Prophet ﷺ said. Allahu akbar, Umar breathed. Then, to lighten the air, he told the whole story of his own wife answering him back, and the Prophet ﷺ smiled, and smiled again, and Umar took heart and sat. He looked around the bare room and could hold it no longer: O Messenger of Allah, make du'a that Allah expands your provision. Persia and Rome live in luxury, and they do not even worship Him; how can you, the chosen one, live like this? The Prophet ﷺ sat up. Are you in doubt, O Umar? Are you not pleased that they have this world and we have the next? Umar's answer was instant: ask Allah to forgive me. Here Dr. Yasir Qadhi pauses the whole episode on that one line, because it answers a question that still gnaws at us: why are the believing nations not the richest? Wealth is not the measure. A people may be given the good of this world precisely because they have no share waiting in the next. True success was lying on that mat.

ayah.arabic: يَا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ قُل لِّأَزْوَاجِكَ إِن كُنتُنَّ تُرِدْنَ الْحَيَاةَ الدُّنْيَا وَزِينَتَهَا فَتَعَالَيْنَ أُمَتِّعْكُنَّ وَأُسَرِّحْكُنَّ سَرَاحًا جَمِيلًا
ayah.text: O Prophet, say to your wives, "If you should desire the worldly life and its adornment, then come, I will provide for you and give you a gracious release.

ayah.arabic: وَإِن كُنتُنَّ تُرِدْنَ اللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُ وَالدَّارَ الْآخِرَةَ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ أَعَدَّ لِلْمُحْسِنَاتِ مِنكُنَّ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا
ayah.text: But if you should desire Allah and His Messenger and the home of the Hereafter, then indeed, Allah has prepared for the doers of good among you a great reward."

paras: What he had done was take an oath, an ila, to stay apart from his wives for a month. The fiqh of it is precise: a husband may swear off intimacy for a fixed time, up to four months, to let a heated dispute cool while both sides think. The Prophet ﷺ used it once, for one month, and lived it out in that bare room.

On the twenty-ninth day he returned. Aisha, counting every day, reminded him he had sworn a month and only twenty-nine had passed; gently he taught her that a month can be twenty-nine days. And then, beginning with her, he laid before each wife the verse Allah had just sent down. He told Aisha not to be hasty, to go and consult her parents before she answered, knowing they would never tell her to leave.

The offer was stark and merciful at once. If you want this world and its comforts, the wealth he truly had access to is yours: take it, with a gracious, gentle release, no bitterness, no harshness, simply parting. But if you want Allah and His Messenger ﷺ and the home of the Hereafter, then Allah has prepared an immense reward for the doers of good among you. Aisha did not even pause. What is there to consult about, she said, when the choice is this clear? I choose Allah and His Messenger ﷺ and the Last Day. She would not even pray over it; some choices are too plain for that. And every single one of the mothers of the believers chose exactly the same, though a fortune was within their reach. Not one of them considered leaving him.

ayah.arabic: إِن تَتُوبَا إِلَى اللَّهِ فَقَدْ صَغَتْ قُلُوبُكُمَا ۖ وَإِن تَظَاهَرَا عَلَيْهِ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ مَوْلَاهُ وَجِبْرِيلُ وَصَالِحُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ ۖ وَالْمَلَائِكَةُ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ ظَهِيرٌ
ayah.text: If you two [wives] repent to Allah, [it is best], for your hearts have deviated. But if you cooperate against him, then indeed Allah is his protector, and Gabriel and the righteous of the believers and the angels, moreover, are [his] assistants.

paras: There is a tender footnote Umar tucks into the story. In the bare room he had said to the Prophet ﷺ: if you ever decide to part from any of your wives, know that Allah is with you, and His angels, and Jibril, and Mikail, and I am with you, and Abu Bakr, and all the believers. Umar used to say that on three occasions Allah sent down revelation agreeing with something he had said, and this was one of them.

For at the opening of Surah at-Tahrim, Allah Himself declared that He is the Prophet's ﷺ protector, with Jibril and the righteous of the believers and the angels behind him. Read in our day it can sound severe, but the Sheikh keeps it where the verse keeps it: a husband loved by the heavens, his back covered by the Lord of all the worlds, even in the privacy of a domestic quarrel. And the door it leaves open is repentance, hearts turning back, the dispute folding into mercy rather than rupture.

When one of the wives quietly hoped he would not tell the others she had chosen him, so that perhaps the rest might choose differently, he ﷺ refused. Allah sent me as a teacher, he said, not as one who makes things hard. He would not use his deeper love for one of them as a reason to be unfair to another. The most beloved marriage in creation, and its fairness held even here.

sections.h2: The lifestyle that proved a prophet
paras: Dr. Yasir Qadhi lands the whole evening on the simplest thing in it. Why would a man with access to the treasures of Persia and Rome choose a palm-fiber mat, and ask the people he loved most to live simply alongside him? Sacrificing for yourself is one thing; asking it of your wives and children is another, and he did it not from poverty but on purpose, to set the standard for an entire ummah. That choice is itself a sign of his prophethood. He was never meant to live as a king, so he lived as a servant, and gave everyone who loved him the same clear choice the verse gives us.

Around that center, the Sheikh gathers what the day teaches about marriage, and asks us to receive it without flinching. In a world where men struck their wives as a matter of course, Aisha swore by Allah that his hand ﷺ never once rose against any woman or servant. When the quarrel grew too hot, it was he who left the home, not she, for you never put a woman out of her house. Cultures differ in how men and women carry themselves, and Islam allows a whole spectrum of that, demanding only that its limits be kept. And a marital problem is not a shame to be buried in silence: the entire city knew his ﷺ home was in difficulty, and he was not embarrassed by it, because problems brought into the light are problems that can be healed.

The best marriage in history was not the one without storms. It was the one whose single serious storm, in a whole lifetime, was weathered so beautifully that we are still learning from it. He gave the mothers of the believers the choice, and to the last of them they chose him, and Allah, and the everlasting home.

dua.translit: Rabbana atina fid-dunya hasanatan wa fil-akhirati hasanatan wa qina adhaban-nar
dua.en: Our Lord, give us good in this world and good in the Hereafter, and protect us from the punishment of the Fire.

lessons_intro: One quarrel, one month, one choice, and the Sheikh draws a whole way of living from it. These threads run straight out of the episode.

lessons.bold: Choose the next world with your eyes open.
lessons.text: Allah laid the two worlds side by side for the mothers of the believers and let them choose. Aisha did not even need to think. When the choice between comfort now and Allah and His Messenger ﷺ is that clear, it is not really a choice at all.
Are you in doubt, O Umar? A people can be handed this world precisely because they have no share in the next. Measure a life by its ethics and its faith, the Sheikh says, not by its GDP.

lessons.bold: His marriage was human, and that is the gift.
lessons.text: The best of men ﷺ married the best of women, and they still had their friction. Allah let it happen, and recorded it, so that we would know how a righteous home weathers a storm.

lessons.bold: When it gets hard, the man leaves, not the woman.
lessons.text: He ﷺ withdrew to a bare room above the masjid; his wives stayed in their homes. In a world that abused women, his hand never once rose against one. Step away to let things cool, the way he did, but never put her out.

lessons.bold: Do not bury a marriage's troubles in shame.
lessons.text: All of Madinah knew his ﷺ home was in difficulty, and he was not embarrassed. Trouble carried into the light, to family or to counsel, is trouble that can be healed.

faq.q: What was the marital dispute about?
faq.a: After wealth began flowing into the ummah, the wives of the Prophet ﷺ asked for a better standard of living, a fairer share of the means he genuinely had access to. Dr. Yasir Qadhi stresses that this was completely permitted and took nothing away from their rank as mothers of the believers. The Prophet ﷺ, however, had chosen for our sake to live simply, so he withdrew from his wives for a month rather than raise his own lifestyle.

faq.q: What is an ila, and how long did the Prophet ﷺ keep it?
faq.a: An ila is an oath a husband takes to stay apart from his wife for a fixed period, up to a maximum of four months, used to let a serious dispute cool while both sides reflect. The Prophet ﷺ used it once in his life, for one month, which he lived out in a small chamber above the masjid. He returned on the twenty-ninth day, teaching Aisha that a month can be twenty-nine days.

faq.q: What is the verse of choice in Surah al-Ahzab?
faq.a: In Surah al-Ahzab 33:28-29, Allah told the Prophet ﷺ to offer his wives a choice: if they wanted the comforts of this world, he would provide for them and release them graciously, with no harshness; but if they wanted Allah and His Messenger ﷺ and the Hereafter, an immense reward awaited the doers of good among them. Beginning with Aisha, every one of the mothers of the believers chose Allah and His Messenger ﷺ.

faq.q: What does the opening of Surah at-Tahrim have to do with this?
faq.a: Surah at-Tahrim 66:4 addresses two of the wives and declares that Allah is the Prophet's ﷺ protector, along with Jibril, the righteous of the believers, and the angels. Umar ibn al-Khattab had said something very similar to the Prophet ﷺ during the dispute, and counted this among the occasions when revelation came down agreeing with him. The verse also leaves the door of repentance open, folding the dispute back toward mercy.

faq.q: Does telling this story diminish the status of the Prophet's ﷺ wives?
faq.a: No, and the Sheikh repeats this carefully throughout. What the wives asked for was halal, the most natural human wish, and seeking it never lowered their noble standing. The episode shows the humanity of a real marriage and ends with every wife choosing Allah and His Messenger ﷺ over a fortune within reach, which is exactly what we would expect of the mothers of the believers.

closing_heading: Why this day stays with you
closing: Of all the days in his life ﷺ, this is the one that lets you breathe. He was not a statue. He had a home with its quiet half-days and its hard nights, and one season when the strain was real enough that he stepped away to a room with a fiber mat and a handful of barley. And out of that very ordinary human ache came something only a prophet would do: he refused to soften his own life, he laid the two worlds bare before the people he loved, and he let them choose freely. They chose him. They chose Allah. They chose the home that does not end.

So let the day ask the same of you that it asked of them. O Allah, make us of those who choose You and Your Messenger ﷺ over every passing comfort, who measure our lives by faith and not by what we own, who carry our homes with the gentleness he carried his, and who are gathered with the mothers of the believers in the home of the Hereafter, the great reward You promised the doers of good. Ameen.

slides:
Day 98 of 104.

In his whole life ﷺ, the marriage reached a breaking point exactly once. Allah sent down Qur'an because of it.

The wives asked for a better standard of living once wealth came in.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi is careful: what they asked was halal. It took nothing from their rank.

But he ﷺ had chosen, for us, to live with less.

A neighbor pounds on Umar's door at night.

Have the enemy attacked?

No. Something worse. The Prophet ﷺ has left his wives.

For them, a crack in his home outweighed an army.

Umar enters the bare room.

A handful of barley. A skin of water. A palm-fiber mat that had pressed its marks into the side of the most honored man ﷺ on earth.

Umar wept.

Make du'a for more, Umar said. Persia and Rome live in luxury.

Are you in doubt, O Umar?

Are you not pleased that they have this world, and we have the next?

Then the verse of choice (al-Ahzab 33:28-29):

Want this world? Take it, with a gracious release.

Want Allah and His Messenger ﷺ and the Hereafter? A great reward awaits.

Aisha did not even pause.

What is there to consult about? I choose Allah and His Messenger ﷺ and the Last Day.

Every wife chose the same. A fortune was within reach. Not one left.

The best marriage in history was not the one without storms.

It was the one whose single storm, in a lifetime, was weathered so beautifully we are still learning from it.

He gave them the choice. They chose him.
social.caption: Day 98 of 104: the one marital dispute of his ﷺ entire life, and the verse of choice. The mothers of the believers asked for more, which was theirs to ask. He withdrew for a month to a room with a fiber mat. Then Allah offered them two worlds, and they chose Him and His Messenger ﷺ. Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's seerah series (episode embedded on the site), Qur'an verified via quran.ai. A new day of his life ﷺ, every day, at buruja.com. #seerah #ProphetMuhammad #Buruja #YasirQadhi #islam #sunnah #marriage

email.subject: The one month he ﷺ withdrew
email.alt_subject: Are you in doubt, O Umar?
email.preview: Day 98: the single marital dispute of his entire life, and the verse that asked his wives to choose.
email.body:
Assalamu alaykum,

In the whole of his life ﷺ, the perfect husband, there was exactly one season when the strain at home grew serious enough that he withdrew. Once. And Allah sent down Qur'an because of it.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi approaches it gently, and so will we. As wealth began to flow into the ummah, the wives of the Prophet ﷺ, the mothers of the believers, asked for a better standard of living. Hold this clearly: what they asked for was halal, the most natural wish in the world, and it took nothing away from their noble rank. But he ﷺ had chosen, for our sake, to live with almost nothing.

So he withdrew for one month to a small room above the masjid. When Umar finally entered it, he found a handful of barley, a skin of water, and a palm-fiber mat that had pressed its marks into the Prophet's ﷺ own side. The most honored man on earth, living like this. Umar wept and said: make du'a for more, the Persians and Romans live in luxury. And the Prophet ﷺ answered: are you in doubt, O Umar? Are you not pleased that they have this world, and we have the next?

Then came the verse of choice. If you want this world, he was told to tell his wives, take it, with a gracious release. But if you want Allah and His Messenger ﷺ and the Hereafter, a great reward awaits. Aisha did not even pause: what is there to consult about? Every one of them chose Allah and His Messenger ﷺ, though a fortune was within reach.

The best marriage in history was not the one without storms. It was the one whose single storm was weathered so beautifully that we are still learning from it.

Read day 98, and watch the Sheikh's full episode, at the link below.
